target.title: fix(config): strip obsolete memorySearch keys before schema validation (#68664)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Obsolete `memorySearch` keys (`chunkSize`, `chunkOverlap`, `maxResults`) in `agents.defaults` cause schema validation to fail because the current migration path moves the root key but fails to strip stale sub-keys before `validateConfigObjectWithPlugins` is called.
- **Why it matters**: This blocks gateway startup for users with legacy configs, triggering a ~39k infinite restart loop over 42 hours in production environments.
- **What changed**: Implemented `agents.defaults.memorySearch-obsolete-keys` migration to strip specific stale sub-keys during the runtime migration pass.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: Internal logic of memory search and per-agent overrides in `agents.list` remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Refactor required for the fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #68664
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: The `memorySearch` sub-keys were deprecated/removed from the Zod schema, but the migration logic only performed a move operation on the top-level `memorySearch` key. If the keys were already present in `agents.defaults`, they bypassed the move migration and hit the validator as `unrecognized_keys`.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Config migration unit tests lacked coverage for partial/obsolete sub-key state within already-migrated root paths.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file**: `src/commands/doctor/shared/legacy-config-migrate.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Configs with `agents.defaults.memorySearch` containing obsolete keys must be stripped and pass validation in a single pass.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Direct unit testing of the migration registry ensures the transformation logic is isolated from IO/Runtime state.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None (internal config cleanup).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[Load] -> [Move Migration (moves root only)] -> [Validation (Fails on sub-keys)] -> [Crash]

After:
[Load] -> [Move + Strip Migration] -> [Validation (Pass)] -> [Gateway Ready]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Windows 11 (PowerShell 7)
- Runtime/container: Node.js v20.x
- Model/provider: N/A (Config layer)

### Steps

1. Create `config.json5` with `agents: { defaults: { memorySearch: { chunkSize: 800 } } }`.
2. Run `openclaw` gateway.
3. Observe `InvalidConfigError: Unrecognized keys: "chunkSize"`.

### Expected

Gateway initializes successfully after migrating keys.

### Actual

Gateway crashes with schema validation error.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets: `test_results.log` (3/3 repro tests passing).

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: Baseline strip, no-op on clean config, combined move+strip.
- **Edge cases checked**: Partial key presence (e.g., only `maxResults` present).
- **What you did not verify**: Upstream CI/CD integration.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes` - automatic cleanup)
- Migration needed? (`Yes` - handled by this PR)
- **If yes, exact upgrade steps**: Automated on first boot post-upgrade.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Migration could theoretically strip valid keys if `MEMORY_SEARCH_OBSOLETE_KEYS` overlaps with future schema.
  - **Mitigation**: Keys are strictly scoped to the `agents.defaults.memorySearch` path.
[test_results.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26860320/test_results.log)

target.title: fix(telegram): reject zero and negative replyToMessageId values
target.body:
## Summary

- `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId` now rejects `0` and negative values in addition to non-numeric inputs
- Telegram message IDs are always positive integers; passing `0` or a negative value would result in a `400 Bad Request` from the Telegram API
- Both the numeric branch (`typeof value === "number"`) and the string branch now enforce `> 0`

## Test plan

- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(0)` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(-1)` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("0")` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("-5")` returns `undefined`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId(42)` returns `42`
- [ ] `normalizeTelegramReplyToMessageId("99")` returns `99`
- [ ] UUID strings return `undefined`
- [ ] Existing `parseTelegramThreadId` behaviour is unchanged

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

## Human Verification (required)

## Review Conversations

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

target.title: fix(gateway): fail fast on missing local probe auth
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a fail-fast in `openclaw gateway status` for the local loopback case: when `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials but neither the persisted token nor an interactive credential can be resolved, the status command synthesises a failed probe with a clear `failureReason` instead of attempting an anonymous WebSocket connection that surfaces as a misleading `Connect: failed`.

Explicit `--url`/`--rpc-url` targets are intentionally exempt: a user pointing at a remote, forwarded, or open gateway should not be blocked by missing local-config credentials.

## Details

- `resolveAuthForTarget` resolves through the probe-surface helper. When the result has neither token nor password AND the target is the known local loopback AND `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials, it falls back to the interactive-surface helper and propagates the failure reason.
- The fail-fast is scoped to `surface === "local" && target.kind === "localLoopback"`. Targets with `kind === "explicit"` (i.e. `--url`/`--rpc-url`) skip the check.
- Tests cover both the local fail-fast path and the explicit-target carve-out, and isolate ambient `gateway.auth.token` env in the probe-auth test to avoid cross-test contamination.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: before this patch, `openclaw gateway status` against a local config with `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` but an unresolvable token (e.g. `secrets.token.env` points at a non-existent env var) would still try an anonymous WebSocket connection and surface `Connect: failed` to the operator. The operator-actionable diagnosis ("missing required local auth") was buried under a misleading network error. After this patch, the probe is short-circuited with a `failureReason` that names the auth shape.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` runtime built from this PR head on a local laptop checkout at `/tmp/wt-68280` (rebased onto `upstream/main` at `8e1b0e61b0`), run against an isolated test config at `/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json` so the live mac-mini gateway and its real auth are not touched. The test config sets `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` and `gateway.secrets.token.env` to an env var that is explicitly unset in the shell.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  unset OPENCLAW_PROBE_NONEXISTENT_TEST_VAR
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from the patched `node dist/index.js` runtime built from this PR head. The first scenario is the local-loopback fail-fast; the second is the explicit-`--url` carve-out.

  ```
  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Missing gateway auth token.",
    "kind": null
  }

  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  {
    "rpc": {
      "ok": false,
      "error": "connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18999",
      "kind": "connect"
    },
    "probeUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:18999"
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: in the local-loopback scenario, `rpc.kind` is `null` (no probe attempt was made) and `rpc.error` names the actionable auth diagnostic instead of a network error. In the explicit-URL scenario, `rpc.kind` is `"connect"` (the probe DID run) and the error is a real network-level `ECONNREFUSED` against the URL the operator supplied. The design boundary holds: missing local SecretRef diagnostics do not leak into the explicit-URL probe contract.

- **What was not tested**: the explicit-URL daemon `--rpc-url` flag was not exercised end-to-end in this proof. The carve-out is structural (`target.kind !== "explicit"` is the only gate) and is covered by the regression tests added in `c6c648a9cd` and `8e1b0e61b0`.

target.title: fix(deepinfra): use OpenAI video end point and tag DeepSeek thinking format
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves Two user-visible DeepInfra integration problems: **Video generation.** Fixes an issue where generating video through the DeepInfra provider used DeepInfra's legacy native inference path and a small, hardcoded set of video models. Newly released DeepInfra video models (Veo 3.1, Wan 2.x, Pixverse, Seedance, …) never appeared, and the video surface didn't behave like OpenClaw's other DeepInfra modalities (chat, image, speech), which already run over DeepInfra's OpenAI-compatible API. DeepInfra has since shipped an OpenAI-compatible `/v1/openai/videos` endpoint and tagged its video models, so the catalog can be discovered live. **DeepSeek replies.** Fixes an issue where chatting with a DeepInfra DeepSeek model (e.g. DeepSeek-V4-Flash) leaked the model's raw reasoning/tool-call markup — `<|DSML|…>` and `reasoning_content` — into the visible reply, and could drop tool calls. Because DeepInfra serves every model family over a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint, OpenClaw treated DeepSeek as a generic "openai" model and never recovered its DeepSeek-dialect output. ## Why This Change Was Made - **Video** now targets DeepInfra's async `/v1/openai/videos` job endpoint (submit → poll → result URL), matching the other OpenAI-compatible surfaces, and builds its model list from live discovery filtered by the `video-gen` tag, with a small static fallback when no API key is configured. Scope: text-to-video only (image/video reference inputs remain unsupported). - **DeepSeek** is declared with `thinkingFormat: "deepseek"` for the `deepseek-ai/*` family (the same per-family dialect approach already used for Qwen), so core strips/recovers DeepSeek's `reasoning_content` and DSML tool calls instead of passing them through; models carrying the `reasoning` / `reasoning_effort` tag are marked reasoning-capable so thinking levels apply. ## User Impact - **Video:** Users get the current DeepInfra text-to-video catalog, auto-discovered (no more stale hardcoded list), generated through the same OpenAI-compatible flow as the other modalities. No config or workflow change needed. - **DeepSeek:** DeepSeek models on DeepInfra now return clean replies — no leaked `<|DSML|…>` / reasoning markup — tool calls work reliably, and reasoning / "thinking" levels are honored. ## Evidence Verified live on a hosted OpenClaw instance running this branch (DeepInfra provider; agent model `deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash`), driven via `openclaw agent`, with outbound HTTP captured at the network layer. **1 — Video uses the new endpoint + tag discovery.** Captured request timeline across two generations: - `GET /v1/openai/models?sort_by=openclaw&filter=with_meta` → 200 — tag discovery (returns 13 `video-gen` models: Veo 3.1/-fast, Wan 2.6/2.2-T2V, Pixverse-6 / T2V / T2V-HD, Seedance 2.0 / 1.5-Pro, Cosmos3, LTX2, p-video) - `POST /v1/openai/videos` → 200 — async submit (returns a `videos_` job) - `GET /v1/openai/videos/videos_` → 200, polled ~every 5 s until succeeded - **zero `/v1/inference` calls**; - MP4s rendered and attached to the reply. _📸 Screenshot: `capture_events` timeline (POST /v1/openai/videos + polls)_ https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1bc82632-378f-4f90-bf4d-f84ac915ff40 **2 — DeepSeek thinking format.** - `deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4-Flash` discovery tags: `["chat","prompt_cache","reasoning"]` - `openclaw agent --thinking high` on a reasoning prompt → correct step-by-step answer with **no `<|DSML|…>` / `reasoning_content` in the reply**, even though the DeepInfra response body did contain a `reasoning_content` field (confirmed in the capture) — i.e. it's recovered, not leaked. _📸 Screenshot: clean DeepSeek reasoning reply_ [request.json](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29214104/request.json) Focused provider tests cover the video submit→poll→result flow (success, immediate-success, job failure, malformed payloads) and the DeepSeek-family tagging.

I did not run a live gateway restart in this read-only review.

target.title: fix(gateway): hold startup-gated requests at server until post-attach (closes #67160)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The openclaw-gateway startup gate from #65365 responds `UNAVAILABLE` to startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`, `sessions.*`, `tools.effective`, `agent.wait`) for the 8–15 s window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration. The Control UI's client-side retry loop (same commit) only helps when the browser already has the retry-aware bundle. Every time an update restarts the gateway, an open browser tab is still running the **pre-fix** bundle, fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red `GatewayRequestError: chat.history unavailable during gateway startup` banner.
- **Why it matters:** Every in-place `openclaw-update` hits this for any user with a chat tab open — which is most of the time.
- **What changed:** The gateway now *holds* gated requests on the server until post-attach finishes, via a small `StartupGateBarrier` (deferred + 20 s timeout) threaded through the request context. If post-attach completes within the wait, the request falls through to the normal handler. If it times out, the existing `retryable/retryAfterMs` `UNAVAILABLE` shape is returned as a fallback. This removes the race independently of what bundle any browser tab has cached.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** UI is untouched. Upstream's declarative startup-gated method set (`STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS`, derived from `startup: true` core descriptors) is left exactly as upstream defines it — this PR adds only the server-side wait, not a competing method list.

## Re-port note (refreshed 2026-05-16)

This PR was originally written on 2026-04-17. Upstream has since restructured the startup gate: `src/gateway/server-startup-unavailable-methods.ts` was **deleted** and the gated method set is now declarative in `src/gateway/methods/core-descriptors.ts`. The change has been **re-architected (not rebased)** onto current `upstream/main` (`210ff7d318`):

- The barrier now lives in a new dedicated `src/gateway/server-startup-gate-barrier.ts` (its previous home no longer exists upstream).
- Upstream's `STARTUP_UNAVAILABLE_GATEWAY_METHODS` is consumed as-is; no hand-maintained list is reintroduced.
- The fail-fast gate in `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` now awaits the barrier, preserving upstream's `GATEWAY_STARTUP_RETRY_AFTER_MS` + `gatewayStartupUnavailableDetails()` response shape on timeout.
- `startupGateBarrier` is threaded as an **optional** field on the post-attach and request-context params, so existing test call sites need no changes and legacy contexts keep the immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #67160
- Related #65322, #65365, #67951
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The startup gate's fail-fast response (`UNAVAILABLE` at 0 ms) makes the race unrecoverable from the client side when the browser-cached UI bundle predates the retry fix in #65365. The retry fix can never help the session that's affected by the update that ships it.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** The gate was designed around "short-lived unavailability the client can retry through." It did not account for long-lived cached bundles that lack the retry code.
- **Contributing context:** #65365 added the gate + client retry in the same commit, so the retry always ships one restart behind the bundle that needs it.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
- **Target test or file:** new `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` (unit), exercising the dispatch seam directly via `handleGatewayRequest`.
- **Scenarios locked in:**
  1. A gated request waits on the barrier and dispatches once the barrier opens (post-attach completes).
  2. A gated request responds with retryable `UNAVAILABLE` only if the barrier does not open within `STARTUP_GATE_WAIT_MS` (20 s).
  3. Non-gated methods bypass the wait entirely.
  4. Contexts without a barrier (legacy) preserve the existing immediate-UNAVAILABLE behavior.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the four dispatch behaviors are exercised directly through `handleGatewayRequest` with a real `createStartupGateBarrier()`; the post-attach release point is a single `barrier.open()` call adjacent to the existing (already-tested) `unavailableGatewayMethods` clear, so it needs no separate harness.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: During the window between `[gateway] ready` and post-attach sidecar registration, the gateway answered startup-gated methods (`chat.history`, `models.list`) with an instant `UNAVAILABLE`. A browser tab still on the prior bundle fires one `chat.history`, gets `UNAVAILABLE`, never retries, and pins the red error banner after every gateway restart. This change holds the request on the server until post-attach instead of failing fast.
- **Real environment tested**: Live `openclaw-gateway` on aarch64 Linux (Snapdragon, WSL2) — real `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway` capture across an `openclaw-update`-triggered restart with a Control UI browser tab open on the prior bundle. Plus the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit driven directly under `node` on the same host.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Restart `openclaw-gateway` with a Control UI tab already open on the prior bundle and follow `journalctl --user -u openclaw-gateway`; separately, run the compiled `createStartupGateBarrier()` from this commit under `node` and drive `waitWithTimeout()` across the timeout, post-attach-`open()`, and already-open paths.
- **Before evidence optional**:

  ```
  05:22:24.767    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.11
  05:22:24.783    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ chat.history  0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  05:22:24.796    [ws] ⇄ res ✗ models.list   0ms errorCode=UNAVAILABLE
  ```

  One request per method, no retries — the cached bundle lacked retry code; red banner pins until reload.
- **Evidence after fix**:

  Real gateway journal, restart-while-tab-open — the server now holds the gated calls ~11 s, then dispatches; zero `UNAVAILABLE`:

  ```
  07:19:26.520    [gateway] ready (10.0s)
  07:19:26.580    [gateway] starting channels and sidecars...
  07:19:30.844    [ws] webchat connected … client=openclaw-control-ui webchat v2026.4.16
  07:19:42.024    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ chat.history 11172ms
  07:19:42.027    [ws] ⇄ res ✓ models.list  11162ms
  07:19:42.111    [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  ```

  Live `node` console output of this commit's compiled barrier:

  ```
  WAIT_MS 20000 isOpen false
  timeout path => false (~121ms)   # held, retryable fallback only after the wait
  open path    => true  (~51ms)    # post-attach open() releases the held request
  already-open => true             # post-attach-complete fast path
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The gated requests are held on the server for ~11.2 s (the exact post-attach window) and then dispatched to the real handler — zero `✗ UNAVAILABLE` rows, no red banner. The compiled barrier holds until `open()` and only falls back to the retryable `UNAVAILABLE` on timeout, matching the live gateway behavior end-to-end.
- **What was not tested**: The gateway journal is from the original landing of this identical barrier code path; this commit re-architects only where the barrier module lives and how it wires into upstream's restructured startup descriptors, not its runtime behavior — which is re-exercised live above under `node`. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring will be captured at deploy via the normal `openclaw-update` flow; a maintainer may apply the `proof:` override if a fresh pre-merge capture is preferred.

## Supplemental verification (not a substitute for the proof above)

- `pnpm build` — clean.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` — clean (full typecheck of sources and tests).
- `src/gateway/server-methods.startup-gate.test.ts` — 4/4 passing (gated→wait→dispatch, timeout→UNAVAILABLE, non-gated passthrough, legacy no-barrier).
- `pnpm check:madge-import-cycles` / `check:import-cycles` — 0 cycles (the barrier module is a zero-import leaf).

## AI disclosure

- [x] AI-assisted (designed and implemented in Claude Code).
- Real behavior proof: the original real-gateway before/after journal (same barrier code path) plus live runtime console output of this commit's compiled barrier — see "Real behavior proof" above. A fresh production restart-while-tab-open journal for the re-architected wiring is captured at deploy time per the scope note; build/typecheck/unit/cycle checks are supplemental only.

target.title: # PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)
target.body:
# PR: Filesystem Access Control (PathGuard)

## Summary

This PR introduces a stricter, policy-driven filesystem access layer (**PathGuard**) for OpenClaw agent filesystem tools, including local media (image/pdf) loaders.

It closes key security and consistency gaps by:
- canonicalizing requested paths before evaluation (realpath-aware, ENOENT-safe),
- preventing traversal and symlink/junction escapes,
- enforcing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths` consistently for classic filesystem tools **and** local media loads,
- preserving **deny-overrides-allow** semantics,
- anchoring workspace-relative policy entries to the workspace root (and re-anchoring after realpath),
- treating `allowedPaths: []` as an explicit **deny-all**,
- supporting absolute glob policy entries (via minimatch) alongside canonical absolute literals,
- returning structured, remediation-focused policy denial tool results (`policy_denied`) when access is blocked,
- and enforcing a monotonic filesystem policy ceiling for spawned subagents (global + agent + subagent defaults + spawn-time tightening), plumbed end-to-end into the spawned run.

**Important scope note:**

R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”

Remote URLs (`http(s)://`) and inline `data:` inputs are not treated as filesystem paths and are outside this layer.

---

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening
- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [x] Tests

---

## Linked Issues

- Closes #39672
- Related #8719

---

## Problem Statement (What was wrong before)

Before this PR, filesystem tool enforcement had multiple gaps:

1) **Path canonicalization and symlink escape handling were fragmented**
- Traversal protections and “what path are we actually touching?” logic were spread across tools/flows.
- Non-existent paths (ENOENT) were tricky: naive realpath calls fail and can allow later-creation escapes.

2) **Policy matching was inconsistent**
- Absolute vs relative entries could be evaluated in different “path spaces”.
- Glob vs non-glob entries could diverge in behavior, especially when “roots” filtering was used as an approximation.

3) **Media tools could diverge from file tool policy**
- Local file references inside image/pdf flows could reopen outside-workspace reads if not enforced per resolved file.

4) **Sandbox and host flows lacked a unified enforcement story**
- Enforcement needed to be identical regardless of whether the run is sandboxed or host-based.

5) **Spawned subagents did not have a guaranteed filesystem policy ceiling**
- Subagents needed an explicit, monotonic ceiling that could not be loosened at spawn time.

---

## What Changed (Design + Implementation)

### 1) Core PathGuard enforcement (`src/security/path-guard.ts`)

PathGuard implements:

- **Canonical resolution for existing paths** via `fs.realpath`.
- **Nearest-existing-parent resolution** for ENOENT paths (prevents “create later via symlink” bypass).
- **Symlink/junction-aware comparisons** in canonical path space.
- **Deny precedence**: deny rules always override allow rules.
- **Workspace-only boundary**: `workspaceOnly` blocks outside-workspace access.
- **Relative entry anchoring**: workspace-relative policy entries are anchored to the workspace root, and re-anchored after realpath resolution.
- **Empty allowlists**: `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit deny-all.
- **Absolute policy entries**:
  - absolute **literals** are canonicalized before comparison,
  - absolute **globs** are supported (matched against canonical real paths) without attempting to realpath the pattern.
- **Glob detection** via `Minimatch(...).hasMagic()` (brace/extglob supported).

### 2) Filesystem tool integration (read/write/edit/apply_patch-style)

- Tool FS config is resolved through the tool/fs policy configuration and enforced using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`.
- Wrappers normalize args and run PathGuard checks on resolved paths.
- `apply_patch` is explicitly guarded by parsing patch hunks and enforcing FS policy on each patch target path (not just args.path/file_path).

### 3) Structured `policy_denied` tool result (model guidance)

When PathGuard denies access, tools return a structured tool result (instead of only throwing a raw error) that:
- states the restriction is system-enforced and cannot be bypassed,
- describes what the tool attempted to do,
- provides actionable remediation options,
- and explicitly points to config keys that can be changed to grant access.

This improves model behavior: fewer useless retries, more direct user remediation.

### 4) Media tool integration (image/pdf local paths)

Local media loads are now policy-checked on the resolved file path:
- `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`
- `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.ts`

Key invariant:
- enforcement is per resolved path using `checkPathGuardStrict(...)`, not via root-prefix approximations.

### 5) Sandbox behavior

For local media loads in sandbox mode:
- PathGuard runs in sandbox flows too,
- policy root uses `sandbox.root` when sandboxed,
- otherwise `workspaceDir` is used.

### 6) Subagent filesystem policy ceiling (spawn-time tightening + runtime enforcement)

Goal: spawned subagents must never receive broader filesystem access than the configured ceiling.

Policy sources (monotonic tightening):
- Global: `tools.fs`
- Per-agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- Subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- Sessions-spawn defaults/ceiling: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (applied when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`)
- Spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge semantics:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: enforced as a ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

Persistence + plumbing:
- Effective policy is persisted on the spawned child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) via `sessions.patch`.
- Gateway plumbs it into the child run as ingress field `toolFsPolicy`.
- Gateway session resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve `spawnedToolFsPolicy` (no widening after reset).
- Embedded runner passes it into tool construction.
- `createOpenClawCodingTools` accepts an `fsPolicy` override and uses it as the effective policy for that run.
- Nested spawns inherit the parent session’s `spawnedToolFsPolicy` ceiling before applying config/tool-call tightening.

---

## Configuration (for contributors)

### Filesystem policy knobs

- `tools.fs.workspaceOnly: boolean`
- `tools.fs.allowedPaths: string[] | undefined` (empty array means deny-all)
- `tools.fs.denyPaths: string[] | undefined`

Subagent ceiling:
- `tools.subagents.fs.{workspaceOnly, allowedPaths, denyPaths}`

Sessions-spawn defaults + spawn-time tightening:
- `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (config default ceiling/tightening)
- `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` (tool arg tightening; can only further restrict)

Notes:
- Deny always wins.
- Relative entries are workspace-anchored.
- Policies are evaluated against canonicalized real paths.

---

## Explicit Non-Goals / Out of Scope

- Exec sandboxing is not implemented here.
  R: “This PR secures filesystem tools; it does not sandbox arbitrary shell execution.”
- No shell argument parsing.
- Remote source governance is out-of-scope.

---

## Implementation Notes (for contributors)

### Policy evaluation edge cases covered

- `allowedPaths: []` is treated as an explicit **deny-all** (not “no restriction”).
- Relative literal entries are **re-anchored after realpath**: if a workspace-relative entry canonicalizes outside the workspace (e.g. it points at a symlink to an external dir), it **does not** match outside-workspace targets.
- Absolute glob entries support symlink-alias authoring by **canonicalizing the non-glob prefix** before matching.
  - When rebuilding the rewritten pattern, the implementation **normalizes slash joining** so root-anchored globs stay semantically equivalent (e.g. `"/**/*.pem"` must not become `"//**/*.pem"`).

### Tool behavior on denial (model guidance)

When a filesystem access is blocked by PathGuard, tools return a structured `policy_denied` result (English text + structured fields) so models:
- do not attempt “bypass retries”,
- clearly communicate what was attempted,
- present actionable remediation options,
- and point to config keys (`tools.fs.*`) for operators to change if access is intended.

### Subagent filesystem ceiling (monotonic tightening)

Effective subagent policy is computed as a ceiling and is monotonic (cannot be widened by spawn calls):
- global: `tools.fs`
- agent: `agents.<id>.tools.fs`
- subagent defaults/ceiling: `tools.subagents.fs`
- sessions-spawn defaults: `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`
- spawn-time tightening: `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` / tool arg `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`

Merge intent:
- `workspaceOnly`: OR
- `denyPaths`: UNION
- `allowedPaths`: ceiling/tightening model (empty list = deny all)

The effective ceiling is persisted on the child session (`spawnedToolFsPolicy`) and forwarded into the embedded runner (`toolFsPolicy` → `createOpenClawCodingTools({ fsPolicy })`).

---

## Verification

- CI checks and tests pass (per latest runs).
- Added/maintained coverage ensuring `tools.sessions_spawn.fsPolicy` is applied as a default tightening when the tool call omits `sessions_spawn.fsPolicy`.
- Verified critical denial scenarios: traversal, symlink/junction escapes, ENOENT parent-walk, deny precedence, empty allowlist deny-all, sandbox media enforcement, apply_patch enforcement, and subagent ceiling propagation.
- Verified monotonic nested spawning: a tightened subagent cannot spawn a child with broader FS access (parent `spawnedToolFsPolicy` is inherited).
- Verified gateway resets (`/new` / `/reset`) preserve the spawned FS ceiling (no widening after reset).

---

## Reviewer / Contributor Notes (Do not regress these invariants)

- Do not replace per-path enforcement with root-prefix approximations for glob policies.
- Keep deny-overrides-allow.
- Keep relative policy entries anchored to workspace (including after realpath).
- Keep sandbox and host local-file enforcement aligned.
- Keep local filesystem policy and remote-source policy separate concepts.
- Keep subagent policy tightening monotonic (no privilege escalation vs ceiling).
- Remember: exec is outside this enforcement layer.

---

## Rollback / Recovery

- Disable restrictions by setting `workspaceOnly: false` and clearing `allowedPaths` / `denyPaths`.
- No data migration required.

target.title: fix(build): add missing config/sessions/store.runtime entry
target.body:
## Problem

Runtime error when session-override loads the session store:

```
Error [ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND]: Cannot find module '/path/to/dist/store.runtime-eZQ7KuiZ.js'
```

## When This Bug Is Triggered

This bug is triggered when users configure **multiple auth profiles** (e.g., rotating between OpenAI and Anthropic providers). Specifically, it occurs in two code paths:

1. **Manual auth profile override** — When a user explicitly selects a specific provider auth profile via session override
2. **Automatic auth profile rotation** — When the system auto-rotates providers based on cooldown policy

In both cases, the system persists the session auth profile override state to disk via `updateSessionStore()`, which triggers the dynamic import of `store.runtime.js`.

Users with a single provider (the default setup) will not hit this bug.

## Root Cause

`src/agents/auth-profiles/session-override.ts` dynamically imports `../../config/sessions/store.runtime.js` via `import()`, which tsdown cannot statically analyze. The entry was missing from `buildCoreDistEntries()`, so the file was not emitted as a standalone chunk.

Introduced in `c0933e2fc8` (2026-03-22, `perf(reply): lazy-load session store writes`).

## Fix

Added a namespaced entry to `buildCoreDistEntries()` in `tsdown.config.ts`:

```ts
"config/sessions/store.runtime": "src/config/sessions/store.runtime.ts"
```

This emits `dist/config/sessions/store.runtime.js` at the path where `session-override.js` expects to find it via relative import (`"./config/sessions/store.runtime.js"`).

## Testing

- `npm run build` emits `dist/config/sessions/store.runtime.js` ✅
- Regression test added in `src/infra/tsdown-config.test.ts` ✅
- Gateway restart confirms error is resolved ✅

Same pattern as #65735 (missing tsdown entries for dynamically-imported runtime modules).

target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

target.title: fix(plugins): iterate all providers in wrapProviderStreamFn
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `wrapProviderStreamFn()` only checked a single provider via `resolveProviderHookPlugin()`. When a built-in provider (e.g. Anthropic) owned the provider ID, external plugins with matching `hookAliases` could never contribute their own `wrapStreamFn`.
- **Why it matters:** External plugins that want to compose stream transformations (e.g. injecting custom tool types into the API payload) on top of a built-in provider's wrapper are silently ignored.
- **What changed:** Applied the same iteration pattern already used by `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin()` and `normalizeProviderConfigWithPlugin()`: check the primary matched plugin first, then iterate all providers from `resolveProviderPluginsForHooks()`.
- **Scope:** Only `wrapProviderStreamFn()` in `src/plugins/provider-runtime.ts`. No other functions changed.

## Change Type

Bug fix — `wrapStreamFn` on external plugins with `hookAliases` is accepted at registration but never called when a built-in provider matches.

## Root Cause

`wrapProviderStreamFn()` called `resolveProviderHookPlugin()` which returns the first matching provider (typically the built-in). The built-in provider's `wrapStreamFn` was used exclusively. External plugins registered via `hookAliases` were discovered by `resolveProviderPluginsForHooks()` but never consulted for `wrapStreamFn`.

The pattern for iterating all providers already existed in `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin()` (line 457) and `normalizeProviderConfigWithPlugin()` (line 486) — `wrapProviderStreamFn` was the only provider hook that didn't follow it.

## Regression Test Plan

Added test: "chains wrapStreamFn from primary provider and hookAlias plugin"

- Registers two providers: a primary (`id: "anthropic"`) and an external plugin (`id: "custom-anthropic"`, `hookAliases: ["anthropic"]`)
- Both have `wrapStreamFn`
- Verifies the external plugin receives the primary's wrapped stream as `ctx.streamFn`
- Verifies the final result is the external plugin's wrapped function

Run: `pnpm vitest run src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts`

## Security Impact

- **Auth/credentials:** No — does not touch auth flows
- **File system:** No
- **Network:** No — stream wrapping is in-process function composition
- **User data:** No

## Human Verification

- Confirmed test passes locally (18/18 in `provider-runtime.test.ts`)
- Confirmed the pattern matches `normalizeProviderTransportWithPlugin` exactly
- Confirmed chaining semantics: each subsequent plugin gets the previous wrapper's output as `ctx.streamFn`

target.title: Test/run state machine clamp guard
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: The `activeRuns` clamp guard in `onRunEnd()` (`Math.max(0, activeRuns - 1)`) was completely untested. Calling `onRunEnd()` more times than `onRunStart()` (e.g. error handling paths, race conditions) would cause `activeRuns` to go negative without this guard.
- Why it matters: A negative `activeRuns` would cause the machine to report incorrect state permanently, potentially breaking busy indicators across all channels.
- What changed: Added two unit tests covering the clamp guard — one for calling `onRunEnd()` with no prior `onRunStart()`, and one for calling it more times than `onRunStart()`.
- What did NOT change: No production code was changed. This is a test-only PR covering an existing defensive guard.

- Missing detection / guardrail: No unit test for mismatched `onRunStart`/`onRunEnd` calls.
- Contributing context (if known): Error handling paths or race conditions can trigger `onRunEnd()` more times than `onRunStart()`.

target.title: Fix/heartbeat task due clock skew
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `isTaskDue` silently fails when system clock moves backward (NTP correction, VM resume, timezone change). `nowMs - lastRunMs` becomes negative so the task never runs.
- Why it matters: Heartbeat tasks get permanently stuck until the clock catches up past `lastRunMs + interval`, which could be 30+ minutes with no error or warning.
- What changed: Added a `lastRunMs > nowMs` guard that treats a future `lastRunMs` the same as `undefined` (never run).
- What did NOT change: All existing behavior for normal clock progression is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)
- Root cause: `isTaskDue` computes `nowMs - lastRunMs` without checking if `lastRunMs` is in the future. A backward clock jump makes this negative, so the condition `>= intervalMs` is never true.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No guard for `lastRunMs > nowMs` (clock skew scenario).
- Contributing context (if known): Can happen on any system after NTP sync, VM resume, or manual clock correction.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/heartbeat.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: `isTaskDue` returns `true` when `lastRunMs > nowMs`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Pure function, no dependencies, single condition check.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None — `isTaskDue` had zero tests before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — test added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
Heartbeat tasks now resume immediately after a clock correction instead of being stuck for up to `interval` minutes.

## Diagram (if applicable)
```text
Before:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs=future -> nowMs - lastRunMs < 0 -> task stuck forever

After:
[clock skew] -> lastRunMs > nowMs -> treat as never-run -> task runs immediately
```

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A

### Steps
1. Set `lastRunMs` to a future timestamp (simulating clock skew)
2. Call `isTaskDue(futureMs, "30m", nowMs)`
3. Observe result

### Expected
- `true` (task should run)

### Actual (before fix)
- `false` (task permanently stuck)

## Evidence
- [x] Failing test before + passing after (new unit tests added)

## Human Verification (required)
- Verified scenarios: clock skew (lastRunMs in future), normal elapsed interval, interval not yet elapsed, undefined lastRunMs
- Edge cases checked: lastRunMs exactly equal to nowMs (returns false, correct — not due yet)
- What you did NOT verify: actual NTP correction on live system

## Review Conversations
- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: Tasks that were intentionally deferred by setting a future lastRunMs would now run immediately.
  - Mitigation: This is not a supported use case — lastRunMs is only ever set to the current time when a task runs.

target.title: fix(msteams): forward messageBack card actions (Action.Submit) to agent (#60952)
target.body:
## Summary

Forward `Action.Submit` messageBack activities to the agent instead of dropping them as empty messages. Closes #60952.

When a user clicks an `Action.Submit` button on an Adaptive Card in Teams, the click is sent as a messageBack activity (`type="message"` with empty text and a `value` object). The message handler strips mentions, finds empty text, and drops the message with "skipping empty message after stripping mentions." The value payload is never forwarded to the agent.

This change adds a check for `activity.value` before the empty-text drop at line 452 of `message-handler.ts`. If the value payload is a non-empty object, it serializes it as `[CARD_ACTION] {json}` so the agent receives the button payload. This is consistent with:

- The `adaptiveCard/action` invoke handling added in #60431 (which handles `Action.Execute`)
- The `fileConsent/invoke` handling added in #64087

The detection pattern: `activity.type === "message" && !text && activity.value && typeof activity.value === "object"`

## Test plan

- [ ] Send an Adaptive Card with `Action.Submit` buttons to a Teams DM
- [ ] Click a button — verify the agent receives `[CARD_ACTION] {"key":"value"}` as the message body
- [ ] Verify normal text messages still work (no regression)
- [ ] Verify empty messages are still dropped (no spurious forwarding)
- [ ] Verify `Action.Execute` invoke path (#60431) is unaffected

## Context

This is the companion fix to #55384/#60431 (Action.Execute). Together they cover both invoke formats Teams uses for Adaptive Card interactions. We've been running a sidecar HTTP proxy workaround for this since March 2026 — this fix eliminates the need for that sidecar for card actions.

target.body:
## Summary
Adds a `runtime.usage` namespace to the plugin runtime, exposing `resolveModelCostConfig()` and `estimateUsageCost()` so context engine plugins can calculate model-cost estimates from OpenClaw's configured pricing instead of hardcoded heuristics.

## Motivation
Context engine plugins, such as [ContextClaw](https://github.com/dodge1218/contextclaw), truncate stale or large context to save tokens. Today they can only estimate savings with a fixed heuristic. OpenClaw already has per-model pricing through `resolveModelCostConfig`; this PR threads those helpers through the plugin runtime so plugins can use the same pricing source as the host.

## Changes
- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts` — adds the runtime usage facade.
- `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` — adds the `usage` namespace to `PluginRuntimeCore`.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` — wires `createRuntimeUsage()` into runtime assembly.
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` — updates the plugin runtime mock.
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts` — asserts the runtime exposes both usage helpers.
- `docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md` — documents the plugin SDK usage helpers.
- `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` — regenerates the public SDK API baseline.

## Real Behavior Proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A plugin can access the new `api.runtime.usage` namespace and call both pricing helpers without importing OpenClaw internals.

- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `feat/plugin-cost-api` at `39b243468f`, after rebasing onto upstream `main` and narrowing the PR to the plugin runtime/docs/test surface.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
pnpm exec tsx .artifacts/pr64436-runtime-usage-proof/run-direct-plugin-api-proof.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from a minimal external plugin fixture with `openclaw.plugin.json` plus `index.mjs`; its `register(api)` calls `api.runtime.usage.resolveModelCostConfig(...)` and `api.runtime.usage.estimateUsageCost(...)`.

```text
[proof] repoHead=39b243468f
[proof] pluginDir=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im
[proof] manifest=/tmp/openclaw-runtime-usage-direct-ile3Im/openclaw.plugin.json
[plugin] id=runtime-usage-proof
[plugin] hasRuntimeUsage=true
[plugin] resolveModelCostConfig cost={"input":3,"output":15,"cacheRead":0.3,"cacheWrite":3.75}
[plugin] estimateUsageCost total=0.010950
[proof] PASS plugin register(api) called both api.runtime.usage helpers
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The plugin saw `api.runtime.usage`, resolved the configured proof model cost, estimated usage cost as `0.010950`, and exited with the PASS line above.

- **What was not tested:** Full OpenClaw app startup and provider billing reconciliation; this proof is scoped to the plugin runtime API surface added by this PR.

## Local Verification
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 node --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `pnpm format:check docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 docs/plugins/sdk-runtime.md src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts src/plugins/runtime/index.ts src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts src/plugins/runtime/runtime-usage.ts src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts -t 'exposes runtime.usage pricing helpers' --reporter=dot`

## Related
- Issue #64085 — Provider circuit breaker cost/status reporting.
- PR #64127 — companion status-provider work.

target.title: Docker: add Mac migration and keep-awake helpers
target.body:
## What
Add operator helpers for Mac-hosted Docker deployments.

## Why
Running OpenClaw in Docker on macOS is easier to operate if the repo includes a simple host-awake helper and a low-disruption backup/restore path for moving from an Intel Mac to Apple Silicon.

## Included
- add `scripts/openclaw-keepawake.sh` to keep the host awake during long-running gateway sessions
- add `scripts/migrate/backup-openclaw.sh` and `scripts/migrate/restore-openclaw.sh` for migration backups
- preserve config, workspace, auth-profile secret-key state, repo `.env`, and Docker setup files
- harden backup archive and checksum file permissions and verify the external archive checksum before extraction
- add a focused migration-helper regression test and Docker docs

## Notes
This PR is intentionally separate from the Docker runtime/image PR so runtime behavior and host-operator helpers can be reviewed independently.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Mac-hosted Docker operator helpers can preserve OpenClaw config, workspace scripts, and auth-profile secret-key state during host migration, and can keep the host awake during long-running gateway sessions.
- **Real environment tested**: Intel macOS host with Docker Desktop and the existing OpenClaw Docker deployment; plus a local restore fixture that exercises the backup/restore scripts without touching the live config.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `bash -n scripts/migrate/backup-openclaw.sh scripts/migrate/restore-openclaw.sh scripts/migrate/lib.sh scripts/openclaw-keepawake.sh`; `pnpm test test/scripts/openclaw-migrate-helpers.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`; `pnpm docs:list`; `node scripts/check-no-conflict-markers.mjs`; `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD`.
- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
bash syntax ok

pnpm test test/scripts/openclaw-migrate-helpers.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
✓ openclaw Docker migration helpers > backs up and restores config, workspace, auth-profile secrets, and archive permissions
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard

docs:list ok (1313 lines)
check-no-conflict-markers passed
git diff --check passed
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The migration test creates a real archive, verifies the companion `.sha256`, proves the archive/checksum are owner-only, restores config/workspace/auth-profile secret files, and verifies restored `.env` permissions. The earlier live Docker proof also confirmed the backup preserves the digest and portfolio workspace scripts used by the deployed gateway.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a destructive restore over the active live config on the same machine; the restore proof uses an isolated temp fixture so the live Docker deployment is not disturbed.

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve indentation when recombining non-streaming replies
target.body:
AI-assisted: yes (OpenClaw agent-authored).
Testing: targeted local validation (gateway regression tests + targeted lint + pre-commit repo checks).

## Summary

- Companion to merged #55960, not a duplicate: #55960 fixed indentation loss while stripping inline reply directives in `src/utils/directive-tags.ts`; this PR fixes the remaining gateway-layer loss when non-streaming reply parts are recombined in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.
- Preserve each non-streaming payload chunk as authored when recombining it, so indentation-sensitive content survives payload boundaries.
- Reuse that same combiner for `/btw` side results so sibling non-streaming gateway paths behave consistently.
- Normalize `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` boundary trimming and add focused regression coverage for the single-newline, `/btw`, and CRLF cases.
- Scope boundary: no streaming-path formatting changes, no Markdown parsing changes, and no channel-specific formatting rules.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #42576
- Related #29377
- Companion to merged #55960

## Relationship to #55960

Merged PR #55960 fixed one real whitespace-loss layer: inline reply-directive stripping in the shared parser (`src/utils/directive-tags.ts`).

This PR fixes a separate remaining layer later in the pipeline: gateway non-streaming recombination of final reply chunks (and `/btw` side-result chunks) in `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`.

So the two PRs are adjacent and complementary, not overlapping duplicates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-streaming final replies and `/btw` side results now preserve indentation when formatted content spans multiple payloads. When one chunk already ends with a single newline and the next chunk does not start with one, the gateway now preserves that authored single newline instead of forcing a blank line. The combiner also trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` cleanly at outer boundaries so Windows-style line endings do not leak stray carriage returns.

This fixes the flattened-output symptom reported against Matrix, but the bug itself is generic to gateway non-streaming recombination of indentation-sensitive content rather than Matrix-specific formatting.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node.js checkout
- Model/provider: n/a (gateway-side reply recombination)
- Integration/channel (if any): reproduced against the gateway non-streaming `chat.send` path; user report was on Matrix
- Relevant config (redacted): n/a

### Steps

1. Produce a non-streaming assistant reply whose formatted block is emitted across multiple final payloads.
2. Include indentation-sensitive content such as nested YAML, a nested list, or a fenced code block.
3. Observe the stored/broadcast final message text.

### Expected

- Indentation from each payload chunk is preserved in the final combined reply.

### Actual

- Leading whitespace at payload boundaries was trimmed away, flattening structured content.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Added focused regression coverage for:
- the authored single-newline separator case
- an indentation-sensitive `/btw` YAML side result
- `CRLF` boundary trimming

The linked user issue includes before/after screenshots of the real-world symptom.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: targeted gateway regression test for the single-newline recombination edge case; targeted `/btw` side-result regression test for an indented YAML block; targeted `CRLF` boundary regression coverage; targeted lint on the changed files; repo pre-commit checks during commit.
- Edge cases checked: whitespace-only payload chunks are still ignored; separator insertion still only happens when adjacent chunks are not already separated by line breaks; authored single-newline separators are preserved instead of expanded to blank lines; `CRLF` boundaries do not leave stray carriage returns.
- What you did **not** verify: live Matrix delivery against a running instance, broader streaming reply behavior outside the existing `/btw` block-side-result coverage, or full-repo test suites.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert commits `6b2461a11`, `c753cd0af`, and `d667f18f1` (or revert the PR branch changes as a unit)
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts`, and `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected extra blank lines between merged payload chunks, unexpected loss of an authored single newline between chunks, stray carriage returns from `CRLF` boundaries, or lost indentation in `/btw` side results

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: preserving authored separators changes the old trim/join behavior for payloads that already end in a single newline.
  - Mitigation: this is intentional, explicitly regression-tested, and still bounded by the same whitespace-only-chunk skipping plus separator insertion only when neither side already provides a line break.
- Risk: preserving chunk text could expose `CRLF` edge cases at outer boundaries.
  - Mitigation: the combiner now trims `CRLF` / `CR` / `LF` consistently at the start/end, with targeted regression coverage.
- Risk: reviewers may mistake this PR for a duplicate of #55960.
  - Mitigation: this description now names the exact layer split: `#55960` fixes directive parsing, this PR fixes gateway non-streaming recombination.

target.title: feat(agents): derive fallbacks from configured model catalog
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw only derives failover candidates from `agents.defaults.model.fallbacks`, so operators who keep multiple models in `agents.defaults.models` still have to duplicate that ordered list manually.
- Why it matters: if the configured primary becomes unavailable, the runtime stops at the primary unless the same catalog is copied into `fallbacks`, which is easy to miss and easy to drift.
- What changed: added an explicit opt-in `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels: true` that derives fallback candidates from `agents.defaults.models` in config order when explicit `fallbacks` are omitted.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not change existing fallback error classification, auth-profile rotation behavior, or the meaning of explicit `fallbacks` / `fallbacks: []`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #60919
- Related #47434
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the fallback resolver only consulted explicit fallback arrays (`fallbacksOverride` or `agents.defaults.model.fallbacks`). `agents.defaults.models` remained allowlist/catalog-only, so keeping multiple configured models there did not influence failover.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no opt-in config path that said “use my configured model catalog as the fallback pool when I omit explicit fallbacks.”
- Contributing context (if known): operators often keep a single ordered model catalog in `agents.defaults.models`, so the duplication requirement was easy to miss.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: derive failover candidates from `agents.defaults.models` only when `fallbacksFromModels: true`, keep explicit `fallbacks` precedence, and keep `fallbacks: []` as an intentional disable.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is isolated to fallback candidate construction before provider execution.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing `runWithModelFallback` unit coverage already covers explicit fallback ordering and allowlist behavior.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New opt-in config: `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels: true`
- When enabled and explicit `fallbacks` are omitted, OpenClaw derives fallback candidates from `agents.defaults.models` in config order, excluding the configured primary.
- Explicit `fallbacks` still win, and `fallbacks: []` still disables fallback.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[primary model fails] -> [check explicit fallbacks only] -> [stop if none configured]

After:
[primary model fails] -> [explicit fallbacks?]
                    -> [if none + fallbacksFromModels=true, derive from models catalog]
                    -> [try next configured model]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (No)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (No)
- New/changed network calls? (No)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (No)
- Data access scope changed? (No)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: fallback candidate construction only (no provider-specific network dependency)
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): `agents.defaults.model.primary`, `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels`, `agents.defaults.models`

### Steps

1. Configure a primary model plus multiple entries in `agents.defaults.models`.
2. Enable `agents.defaults.model.fallbacksFromModels: true` without setting explicit `fallbacks`.
3. Trigger a failover-worthy primary error.

### Expected

- The next configured model from `agents.defaults.models` is attempted.

### Actual

- Before this PR, the run stopped at the primary unless the fallback list was duplicated manually.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - new unit coverage for catalog-derived fallback
  - explicit `fallbacks` still override catalog derivation
  - explicit `fallbacks: []` still disables fallback
  - config schema + baseline regeneration
- Edge cases checked:
  - configured primary is excluded from derived catalog fallbacks
  - duplicate catalog keys are deduped before candidate construction
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repository `build` / `check` green, because latest `main` is already red on unrelated `skills/SourceInfo` and `provider-transport-fetch` issues
  - full `src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts` run without unrelated unhandled plugin-registration/import noise on current `main`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (Yes)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: operators may assume the models catalog now always implies fallback.
  - Mitigation: the behavior is explicit opt-in (`fallbacksFromModels: true`) and docs clarify that explicit `fallbacks` still take precedence.
- Risk: derived catalog order may surprise users if they keep a broad manual allowlist.
  - Mitigation: opt-in only, preserves config order, and `fallbacks: []` remains the escape hatch to disable fallback.

## Validation notes

- `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts -t 'derives fallbacks from configured models when enabled|prefers explicit fallbacks over configured model derivation|treats explicit empty fallbacks as disabling configured model derivation'`
  - 3 targeted tests passed
  - current `main` still emits unrelated unhandled plugin registration/import errors during this file
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/schema.base.generated.test.ts`
  - passed (`2` files / `6` tests)
- `pnpm build`
  - fails on existing upstream errors in `src/agents/provider-transport-fetch.ts`, `src/agents/skills/local-loader.ts`, `src/agents/skills/skill-contract.ts`
- `pnpm check`
  - fails on the same existing upstream `skills/SourceInfo` errors

target.title: feat(extension): support session cache for volcengine
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Volcengine provider lacked session-based response caching support for multi-turn conversations. - Why it matters: Without session caching, every turn sends full context to the API, increasing latency and cost for Volcengine Doubao models. - What changed: Added extensions/volcengine/cache.ts with session cache logic — manages previous_response_id, caching, thinking, store, and expire_at fields automatically. Configured via params.cache: { enable: true, ttlSec?, thinking? }. Wired into the provider via wrapStreamFn in index.ts. (cache.test.ts, 27 tests). Updated docs with config examples. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Provider registration, catalog, auth flow unchanged. No changes outside extensions/volcengine/ and docs. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). Once enable cache and openai-responses, we can see the cache read token from session file for each LLM request config example： ``` "models": { "providers": { "volcengine": { "api": "openai-responses" } } }, "agents": { "defaults": { "models": { "volcengine/xxxx": { "alias": "volcengine", "params": { "cache": { "enable": false, "ttlSec": 3600, "thinking": true } } } } } } ``` session file example: ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.
